title: Cross-tenant metadata disclosure via unqualified pg_catalog reads, advertised in the system prompt; SQL validator gaps where SET ROLE is the only defense
claim: SQLValidator only checks schema-qualified table references; unqualified pg_namespace/pg_class/pg_views/pg_tables resolve via the implicit pg_catalog search_path entry and are world-readable in PG16 regardless of SET ROLE, and tenant schema names are derived from tenant.external_id (customer/domain identifiers) with pg_class.reltuples leaking other tenants' approximate row counts — the system prompt explicitly tells the agent these catalog views are reachable, so any chat user or prompt-injected content can enumerate the shared DB's customer list via the query tool. _validate_statement_type also checks only the top-level expression type, so SELECT ... INTO and data-modifying CTEs (WITH x AS (INSERT...) SELECT) pass, and pg_sleep/set_config are absent from DANGEROUS_FUNCTIONS — defense-in-depth erosion currently backstopped only by the _ro role grants/PG16 defaults. The view-schema _ro role also retains SELECT/USAGE on removed tenants' schemas after a rebuild (no revoke), a latent cross-tenant read primitive fenced only by the validator's schema allowlist.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain independently.

PRIMARY (cross-tenant metadata disclosure, BROKEN-NOW):
1. sql_validator.py:269-288 `_validate_table_access` only enforces the schema allowlist `if table_schema:` — i.e. only when `exp.Table.db` is set. I ran sqlglot myself: `SELECT relname, reltuples FROM pg_class` and `SELECT nspname FROM pg_namespace` yield Table nodes with db=None, so the allowlist is skipped and they pass validation.
2. query.py:44-50: `SET ROLE {readonly_role}` then `SET search_path TO {schema_name}`. pg_catalog is always implicitly searched in PostgreSQL regardless of search_path, and pg_class/pg_namespace/pg_views/pg_tables have default SELECT-to-PUBLIC grants. I grepped the whole workspaces/ and mcp_server/ tree — there is NO `REVOKE ... FROM PUBLIC` and no catalog hardening; the only REVOKEs (schema_manager.py:535,541,573,579) run inside _drop_readonly_role during TEARDOWN, never on the catalogs. So the _ro role reads every row of pg_class/pg_namespace = all tenant schemas + reltuples.
3. schema_manager.py:66 `schema_name = self._sanitize_schema_name(tenant.external_id)` and _sanitize_schema_name (625-631) just lowercase/underscore the external_id — so pg_namespace enumeration leaks the customer identifier list; pg_class.reltuples leaks other tenants' approx row counts.
4. base_system.py:167 verbatim: 'Unqualified `pg_catalog` views (`pg_namespace`, `pg_class`, `pg_views`, `pg_tables`) are reachable if you really need raw system-state introspection'.
5. Reachability live: chat/views.py:155 `mcp_tools = await get_mcp_tools()` (loads the `query` tool), passed to build_agent_graph (171/183), bound via llm.bind_tools (graph/base.py:522); system prompt built at graph/base.py:724 includes BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT. The MCP `query` tool (server.py:334) calls execute_query (query.py:106) -> validator. A chat user or prompt-injected content can have the agent run an arbitrary SELECT against the catalogs.

SECONDARY (latent / defense-in-depth, exactly as the finding frames them):
- _validate_statement_type (sql_validator.py:221-246) checks only the top-level expression. My sqlglot trace confirms `WITH x AS (INSERT INTO foo VALUES (1) RETURNING id) SELECT * FROM x` and `SELECT * INTO newtab FROM pg_class` parse as top-level exp.Select and pass type validation (the INSERT/INTO targets are unqualified Tables so table-access is also skipped). Backstopped by the _ro role lacking write privileges.
- DANGEROUS_FUNCTIONS (27-91) omits pg_sleep and set_config — confirmed by reading the full frozenset; pg_sleep is capped by the 30s statement_timeout (query.py:49).
- build_view_schema (schema_manager.py:391-405) grants USAGE/SELECT only to current tenant schemas and never revokes a removed tenant's grants on rebuild; _view_schema_name (214-217) is deterministic so ws_<hash>_ro persists across rebuilds and accumulates grants. Rebuild is dispatched from workspace_service.py:67-71 on tenant removal. Fenced by the validator's schema allowlist (public + current schema only), so latent.

Every quote and line number in the finding checks out against the actual files.
votes.notes: The headline disclosure is genuinely exploitable today: validator gap + PG16 catalog-to-PUBLIC defaults + search_path implicitly including pg_catalog + schema-names-from-external_id, all reachable through the live chat -> query MCP tool. The disclosed data is metadata (tenant/customer schema names and approximate row counts), not raw row data, so the blast radius is customer enumeration + sizing, not full data exfiltration — still a real cross-tenant info-disclosure. One nuance: the system prompt is an aggravating/legitimizing factor, not the root cause — even with no prompt mention, a user can craft the SELECT directly because the validator is the actual boundary and it has the gap (the finding's own wording, 'SET ROLE is the only defense', captures this correctly). The prompt is also internally contradictory: lines 136-157 ('When the Schema is Broken') tell the agent NOT to query these catalogs, while line 167 says they're reachable. The bundled secondary items (data-modifying CTE/SELECT INTO, pg_sleep/set_config, residual view-role grants) are correctly LATENT/defense-in-depth as the finding states; only the pg_catalog disclosure is BROKEN-NOW.

title: Loaders follow server-supplied next URLs anywhere with session-pinned credentials (no host/scheme validation)
claim: connect_base and ocs_base take the next request URL from the response body; auth lives in session headers which requests sends to whatever host/scheme that URL names (header stripping applies only to redirects, not body-supplied URLs), and there is no host/scheme validation. Production Connect already returns scheme-downgraded http:// next URLs (acknowledged in code). A compromised/misbehaving provider could redirect credentialed requests to an arbitrary host. Related unchecked TODO.md item: loader/export network egress is unrestricted.

votes.evidence:
Independently reproduced the full chain by reading the files.

(1) Body-supplied next URL, no validation:
- mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:234 `url = payload.get("next")`, looped back into `self._session.get(url, params=request_params, headers=headers, timeout=HTTP_TIMEOUT)` at line 195. No host/scheme check anywhere in `_paginate_export_pages`.
- mcp_server/loaders/ocs_base.py:84 `current_url = payload.get("next")`, looped back into `self._session.get(current_url, ...)` at line 68. No validation in `_paginate`.

(2) Session-pinned credentials sent to any host:
- connect_base.py:127 `self._session.headers.update({"Authorization": f"Bearer {credential['value']}"})`.
- ocs_base.py:41-43 sets `X-api-key` or `Authorization: Bearer`.
requests.Session merges session headers into every prepared request (merge_setting) with no host comparison; host-based stripping (rebuild_auth/should_strip_auth) only runs inside resolve_redirects for followed 3xx redirects, not for a fresh .get() to a body-supplied URL. The finding's mechanism is exactly right.

(3) Acknowledged production downgrade: connect_base.py:187-194 comment documents production Connect returning `http://` next URLs (commcare-connect#1109).

(4) Reachability — live, not dead code: loaders instantiated in mcp_server/services/materializer.py (imports lines 60-72; dispatch table line 789+), run inside the `materialize_workspace` procrastinate task (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:204, `@task(pass_context=True)`), enqueued from two live triggers: mcp_server/server.py:607 `materialize_workspace.defer_async(...)` (MCP tool) and apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185 (API route).

(5) TODO corroboration: TODO.md:41 `- [ ] **Network isolation for loaders** — restrict loader subprocess egress to configured API endpoints only` (unchecked).
votes.notes: Cited line numbers connect_base.py:234, ocs_base.py:84, envelope.py:107 are accurate. dbt_runner.py:28 (generate_profiles_yml) is tangential — relevant only to the broader egress theme, not the credential-leak chain. Strengthening nuance (not a correction): the already-live http:// downgrade means the token is sent over cleartext on the first hop to the legitimate host before the edge 301s to https — a real-now minor exposure — but the headline arbitrary-host escalation is correctly classified LATENT since it needs a compromised/misbehaving upstream. Status LATENT and impact security are both accurate.

title: CommCare offset pagination under live writes can silently skip records
claim: The CommCare forms loader walks tastypie limit/offset pages with no explicit ordering; submissions arriving mid-walk shift offsets — duplicates are absorbed by ON CONFLICT (but inflate the recorded row count via total += len(page)), while skips are silent and permanent until the next full reload. Connect's keyset design avoids this; CommCare never got an equivalent watermark.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain.

REACHABILITY (live): `forms` is a registered source in `pipelines/commcare_sync.yml:9` ("CommCare form submission records"). The pipeline runs via the Procrastinate task `materialize_workspace` (`apps/workspaces/tasks.py:203-204`) → `run_pipeline` (`materializer.py:96`, iterates `pipeline.sources` at `materializer.py:259`) → `_load_source` (`materializer.py:763-765`):
```
if source_name == "forms":
    loader = CommCareFormLoader(domain=domain, credential=credential)
    return _write_forms(loader.load_pages(), schema_name, conn, on_page=on_page)
```
Note `load_pages()` is called with NO cursor argument — contrast the resumable Connect path at `materializer.py:804`: `pages = loader.load_pages(start_last_id=start_cursor)`.

NO ORDERING / OFFSET PAGINATION: `commcare_forms.py:44-71` sends only `params = {"limit": self.page_size}` on the first request (no `order_by`/sort), then follows `data.get("meta", {}).get("next")`. The `meta.next` URLs are tastypie offset links — confirmed by `commcare_base.py:55` docstring ("Query-string-only (e.g. ``?limit=1000&offset=1000``)") and by the test fixtures in `tests/test_commcare_forms_loader.py` (e.g. line 52: `"next": "/a/dimagi/api/v0.5/form/?offset=2&limit=2"`). grep for `order_by` across `commcare_forms.py`, `commcare_cases.py`, `commcare_base.py` returns nothing. So pages are walked by raw offset with no stable sort key — submissions arriving mid-walk shift offsets, producing duplicates and/or skips.

COUNT INFLATION + SILENT SKIPS: `_write_forms` (`materializer.py:1226-1227`):
```
cur.executemany(ins_sql, rows)
total += len(page)
```
`ins_sql` = `_FORMS_INSERT` (`materializer.py:1098-1108`) = `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (form_id) DO UPDATE SET ...`. So a duplicate form_id returned after an offset shift is absorbed by ON CONFLICT (no error, upsert) yet STILL counted via `total += len(page)` — inflating the returned/reported row count. A skipped form is never fetched and is silently absent until the next full reload (forms is full DROP/CREATE each run — `materializer.py:1189` `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS {}.raw_forms CASCADE`).

CONNECT CONTRAST (keyset/watermark): `connect_base.py:147-237` `_paginate_export_pages` uses `start_last_id` / `last_id` keyset pagination — docstring at line 160-162: "Connect's keyset pagination resumes with records strictly greater than that id (forward order)." `_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES` (`materializer.py:772`) drives cursor-checkpointed resumes. CommCare forms (and cases, `materializer.py:760-762`) have no equivalent watermark — exactly as the finding states.

Every load-bearing element of the claim reproduced: offset walk, no ordering, ON CONFLICT absorbs dups, `total += len(page)` inflates count, silent permanent skips until full reload, Connect has keyset and CommCare does not.
votes.notes: Status DEBT (not BROKEN-NOW) is correct: skips/duplicates only manifest when writes land mid-walk, and only between the page already passed and the current offset — probabilistic, not deterministic. Two minor refinements, neither changing the verdict: (1) tastypie's default ordering is unspecified rather than literally random; the hazard is that without an explicit `order_by` the server makes no stability guarantee across offset windows, which is the precise mechanism the finding describes. (2) The same defect applies to the `cases` source (`commcare_cases.py` follows `data.get("next")` offset links with no ordering, written via `_write_cases` ON CONFLICT (case_id) DO UPDATE, `materializer.py:1171-1172`) — the finding scopes itself to forms but the cases loader is identically affected, so if anything the blast radius is slightly understated. impact=correctness and the file:line anchors (commcare_forms.py:44, materializer.py:1226) are both accurate.

title: Mid-rematerialization reads: Connect serves silently-partial tables while CommCare/OCS block queries until 30s timeout
claim: The tenant schema stays ACTIVE during re-materialization and context serves it. Connect writers commit DROP+CREATE immediately then grow tables page by page, so concurrent agent queries return empty/partial tables with no warning; CommCare/OCS writers hold DROP inside one transaction, so concurrent queries block on ACCESS EXCLUSIVE until the 30s statement_timeout for the duration of an hours-long load. Two inconsistent, uncommunicated failure modes for the same situation.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain on the live code (not the worktree copy).

ENTRY / SCHEMA STAYS ACTIVE: run_pipeline (materializer.py:183) calls SchemaManager().provision(tenant). provision() (schema_manager.py:66-78) derives the STABLE name `t_<id>` from tenant.external_id and, when an ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING record already exists, does `existing.touch(); return existing` with NO state change. run_pipeline never flips the record to MATERIALIZING during the rewrite — it only sets `tenant_schema.state = "active"` at the very end (line 485-486). load_tenant_context (context.py:56-59) serves any schema in `state__in=[ACTIVE, MATERIALIZING]`, so the live schema is served throughout the rewrite. The `query` MCP tool (server.py:334-351) resolves this context and connects with `options=...statement_timeout=30000` (context.py:159).

IN-PLACE, NOT BLUE-GREEN: create_refresh_schema / refresh_tenant_schema (the `_r<hex>` non-destructive path) exist ONLY in `.claude/worktrees/integration-incident-fixes/` — grep over live apps/ finds no caller. The live path is materialize_workspace (tasks.py:204) -> _run_pipeline_with_progress (tasks.py:466) -> run_pipeline -> provision() in-place on `t_<id>`.

CONNECT = SILENTLY PARTIAL: _write_connect_visits commits immediately after DROP+CREATE (materializer.py:1450) and again per page (line 1490). _load_connect_source drives resumable sources (visits/completed_works/payments/invoices/assessments/completed_modules, line 772-774) with resumable=True; _run_writer/_load_and_commit_source then SKIPS the outer commit for resumable (line 713 `if not resumable: conn.commit()`). Net: a reader sees a committed empty table immediately, then partial rows growing page by page, no warning.

COMMCARE/OCS = BLOCK TO TIMEOUT: _write_cases (1122), _write_forms (1189), _write_ocs_experiments (851), _sessions (900), _messages (961), _participants (1017) each issue `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ... CASCADE` then CREATE+INSERTs with NO internal conn.commit(). _load_and_commit_source (676-714) sets `conn.autocommit = False`, runs the whole writer, and commits ONCE at line 714. So DROP holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE uncommitted for the entire (hours-long, N+1 for OCS messages) load; a concurrent SELECT (ACCESS SHARE) blocks until commit or its 30s statement_timeout fires.

REACHABILITY (live today): re-materialization is dispatchable with no re-run guard via HTTP (apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185 `materialize_workspace.defer_async`) and via the agent-callable MCP tool run_materialization (server.py:520, dispatch at 607). Concurrent reads arrive through the `query` MCP tool (server.py:334). Both DROP behaviors and the ACTIVE-serving read path are wired and reachable.

All three cited file:line anchors check out (materializer.py:1418-1450 = Connect DROP-guard + immediate commit; context.py:58 = ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING serve filter, context.py:159 = 30s timeout; schema_manager.py:73 = existing-ACTIVE short-circuit that keeps the schema ACTIVE).
votes.notes: Mechanism, severity (LATENT/correctness), and reachability are all accurate. Two inconsistent uncommunicated failure modes for the identical situation is real. Minor severity nuances for the synthesizer: (1) Connect-users is non-resumable (excluded at line 770-774 because rows are mutable), so within a single Connect re-materialization the `users` table behaves like the CommCare/OCS blocking mode while the other six Connect tables behave like the partial mode — so even Connect alone is internally inconsistent. (2) The OCS-messages blocking window is the worst case: its loader does N+1 per-session detail fetches all inside the one uncommitted transaction, so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE hold can be extremely long. (3) replication:1 — only one reviewer flagged it, but I could not refute any element. (4) No advisory lock per tenant_schema (noted in server.py:583 comment), and two threads in the same workspace can even dispatch parallel in-place rewrites of the same tables, compounding the window.

title: purge_synced_data orphans WorkspaceViewSchema rows and ws_* physical schemas
claim: The purge_synced_data command tears down all tenant schemas and deletes TenantSchema rows but never touches WorkspaceViewSchema: physical ws_* schemas survive (emptied of views by CASCADE) and their rows keep their prior state (typically ACTIVE) over hollow schemas. Dev-targeted operator command.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain independently.

ENTRY POINT (reachable): apps/workspaces/management/commands/purge_synced_data.py is a real Django management command (help text: "Purge all materialized tenant data"). It's invocable via `manage.py purge_synced_data --confirm` and is wired into an invoke task at /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/tasks.py:106 (`c.run("uv run python manage.py purge_synced_data --confirm", pty=True)`). Dev/operator-targeted, as the finding states.

WHAT IT TEARS DOWN: handle() iterates `for tenant_schema in TenantSchema.objects.all()` and calls `manager.teardown(tenant_schema)` (line 51). SchemaManager.teardown (schema_manager.py:194-212) runs `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {tenant_schema.schema_name} CASCADE`. It then deletes all TenantSchema rows (line 59), all TenantMetadata rows (line 60), and runs `Workspace.objects.update(data_dictionary=None, ...)` (line 61).

WHAT IT NEVER TOUCHES — confirmed two distinct gaps:
1. WorkspaceViewSchema ROWS: The command never references WorkspaceViewSchema at all (it's not even imported — line 7 imports only TenantMetadata, TenantSchema, Workspace). It does NOT delete Workspace rows, so the `WorkspaceViewSchema.workspace = OneToOneField(Workspace, on_delete=CASCADE)` (models.py:222-226) never fires. Rows keep their prior `state` (default/typical ACTIVE per build_view_schema line 436).
2. ws_* PHYSICAL SCHEMA: The view-schema teardown method `teardown_view_schema` (schema_manager.py:449) / `ateardown_view_schema` (line 494) — which would `DROP SCHEMA ws_* CASCADE` — is never called by this command. The ws_* schema name is distinct from tenant schemas: `_view_schema_name` returns `f"ws_{hex_id}"` (line 217).

CASCADE-EMPTYING IS ACCURATE: Views in ws_* are built as `CREATE VIEW {ws_schema}.{view} AS SELECT * FROM {tenant_schema}.{table}` (schema_manager.py:369-374). Dropping the tenant schema with CASCADE drops these dependent views in the ws_* schema, but the ws_* schema container itself survives — exactly "physical ws_* schemas survive (emptied of views by CASCADE)" per the finding.

TEST CONFIRMATION: tests/test_purge_synced_data.py only ever patches/asserts `SchemaManager.teardown` (lines 83-117) — never teardown_view_schema and never asserts WorkspaceViewSchema cleanup, confirming the command genuinely has no view-schema handling.

NET RESULT: After purge, a hollow ws_* schema (no views) is left behind alongside a WorkspaceViewSchema row still claiming ACTIVE — a real orphan/state inconsistency, matching the finding precisely.
votes.notes: Severity/status (DEBT, correctness) is accurate and arguably even slightly generous in the right direction. Mitigating context that keeps this at DEBT rather than higher: (1) the command is explicitly dev/operator-targeted; (2) the inconsistency is self-correcting on next materialization — provision() and build_view_schema() use get_or_create on WorkspaceViewSchema (schema_manager.py:280) and `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS ws_* CASCADE` + `CREATE SCHEMA` (lines 358-365), so a subsequent rebuild reconciles both the stale row and the hollow physical schema. No data-loss (source data is in the tenant projects, not Scout's managed DB) and no security exposure (the ro role for ws_* is also left dangling but grants access only to an empty schema). So correctness is the right impact axis. One nuance not in the finding: the readonly role `ws_*_ro` is also orphaned alongside the schema, a minor additional debt.

title: Dependency graph between tenant schemas and view schemas has no owner: reconciled by 6+ hand-maintained hooks, four live mutation paths lack required hooks
claim: The platform-side dependency graph (WorkspaceTenant) and physical-side graph (pg_depend via DROP CASCADE) are reconciled by at least six hand-maintained hooks scattered across materialize_workspace, teardown_schema, workspace_service, the resume task, chat touch, and MCP context; four live DDL-mutating paths lack required hooks (refresh, MCP teardown tool, transformations trigger, purge command) and the TTL keep-alive clause has five inconsistent implementations (only chat's touch_workspace_schemas is complete; MCP/recipes/artifacts/transformations under-touch, so a multi-tenant workspace kept alive only via non-chat surfaces expires its constituent schemas and the view schema flips FAILED while in active use). Each incident fix (#227-#230) added another hook at another site instead of a choke point — the contract is enforced by convention, already violated four times. When only the view schema expires (tenant data intact), the agent's only recovery is a full run_materialization re-sync because no tool/endpoint exposes the cheap rebuild_workspace_view_schema.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full dependency-graph reconciliation and every claimed gap. The contract: tenant schemas (t_<id>) are SHARED across workspaces and underlie multi-tenant WorkspaceViewSchemas (ws_<hash>), whose views are CREATE VIEW ws.x AS SELECT * FROM t_<id>.tbl (schema_manager.py:367-375). A DROP SCHEMA ... CASCADE on a tenant schema cascade-drops those views (schema_manager.py:198), so every tenant-mutating/teardown path must reconcile dependents.

RECONCILIATION HOOKS (hand-maintained, scattered — confirmed 6+):
1. materialize_workspace builds view schema post-loop (tasks.py:322-336) AND defers sibling rebuilds (tasks.py:346, _rebuild_sibling_view_schemas:441-463).
2. teardown_schema task calls _fail_dependent_view_schemas (tasks.py:653, 666-687) to flip ACTIVE dependent view schemas to FAILED after a tenant DROP.
3. workspace_service.add/remove_workspace_tenant (workspace_service.py:22-71) re-PROVISIONs/tears down view schemas on membership change.
4. chat touch_workspace_schemas (workspace_service.py:74-110) touches view schema + ALL constituent tenant schemas.
5. MCP load_workspace_context touches view schema only (context.py:125).
6. provision() resets TTL on resurrect (schema_manager.py:120-122).

FOUR LIVE DDL-MUTATING PATHS LACKING REQUIRED HOOKS — all confirmed reachable today:
(a) refresh_tenant_schema (tasks.py:126-200): provisions+materializes a fresh tenant schema then tears down OLD ones (DROP SCHEMA, tasks.py:188-197) but never calls _rebuild_sibling_view_schemas nor _fail_dependent_view_schemas — sibling multi-tenant view schemas are left stale.
(b) MCP teardown_schema tool (server.py:801-865): ateardown_view_schema + ateardown of all the workspace's tenant schemas, but NO _fail_dependent_view_schemas for SIBLING workspaces sharing those tenants — unlike the teardown_schema task. Reachable: it is in MCP_TOOL_NAMES (base.py:73), exposed to the agent.
(c) transformations trigger (apps/transformations/views.py:121-159, route POST /api/transformations/runs/trigger/, app installed config/settings/base.py:80): runs run_transformation_pipeline (executor.py:32-93) which runs dbt with +materialized:"table" (dbt_project.py:44) against ts.schema_name — DDL recreating tables in the shared tenant schema — yet never rebuilds dependent view schemas. Touch is single-tenant only (views.py:146, ts.touch()).
(d) purge_synced_data command (purge_synced_data.py:49-61): manager.teardown() (DROP CASCADE) on every tenant schema then deletes TenantSchema rows, but never fails/deletes WorkspaceViewSchema rows — they stay ACTIVE pointing at dropped views. (Dev-only command — see notes.)

FIVE INCONSISTENT TTL KEEP-ALIVE IMPLEMENTATIONS — confirmed, only chat is complete:
- chat (workspace_service.py:96-110): view schema + ALL constituent tenant schemas. COMPLETE.
- MCP load_workspace_context (context.py:113-125): touches ONLY vs.atouch(); constituent tenant schemas NOT touched. UNDER-TOUCHES.
- recipes (apps/recipes/api/views.py:113-122): touches only workspace.tenant (single tenant). UNDER-TOUCHES for multi-tenant.
- artifacts (apps/artifacts/views.py:795-812): tenants.afirst() → load_tenant_context → touches that one tenant schema only. UNDER-TOUCHES for multi-tenant.
- transformations (views.py:140-146): single tenant schema only. UNDER-TOUCHES.

MECHANISM holds: tenant schemas and the view schema carry independent last_accessed_at. A multi-tenant workspace exercised only via MCP/recipes/artifacts/transformations leaves the constituent tenant schemas' last_accessed_at stale → expire_inactive_schemas (cron */30, tasks.py:516-544) flips them TEARDOWN → teardown_schema DROP CASCADE destroys the views → _fail_dependent_view_schemas (tasks.py:653) flips the still-ACTIVE view schema to FAILED while it is in active use.

NO CHEAP-REBUILD RECOVERY EXPOSED: MCP_TOOL_NAMES (base.py:65-76) = {list_tables, describe_table, query, get_metadata, run_materialization, get_schema_status, teardown_schema, get_lineage}. There is NO tool/endpoint exposing rebuild_workspace_view_schema. When only the view schema is FAILED (tenant data intact), the agent's only path is run_materialization (full re-sync). CONFIRMED.
votes.notes:
Verdict CONFIRMED. The "6+ hooks / four missing / five inconsistent touches / no cheap-rebuild tool" framing is all independently reproduced from the code. Two calibration notes, neither of which weakens the DEBT/velocity classification:

1. Severity of gap (d) is lower than (a)-(c): purge_synced_data is an explicitly dev/operator-only management command ("purge ... from the dev environment"), run manually, that deletes ALL TenantSchema rows and clears data dictionaries wholesale. It leaves orphan WorkspaceViewSchema rows ACTIVE, but it is not a per-user runtime path and a full purge is already a known destructive reset. Counting it as one of the "four live mutation paths" is fair (it does DROP CASCADE without the dependent-fail hook) but it is the weakest of the four for production blast radius.

2. The finding's headline status DEBT/velocity is the right altitude: the load-bearing claim is the architectural one — the platform/physical dependency graph has no single owner and is reconciled by convention across 6+ sites, already violated 4 times, with each incident fix (#227-#230) adding another site rather than a choke point. That is genuinely DEBT/velocity. The under-touch paths additionally constitute a real latent correctness/availability bug (a workspace in active use can have its query layer flip FAILED), so an argument exists for LATENT/correctness on that sub-issue; but since the finding explicitly scopes itself to the missing-owner contract problem and files the consequence as supporting evidence, DEBT/velocity is defensible and I did not downgrade. No refutation found on any sub-claim.

title: Status/catalog derivation implemented ~7 ways with concrete user-visible divergence (last_synced_at COMPLETED-only vs prompt/get_schema_status COMPLETED|PARTIAL); 'usable' view-schema predicate diverges during rebuild
claim: Seven independent derivations of materialization/schema status exist (resume aggregate, jobs polling, refresh-status, workspace detail, two MCP tools, two prompt builders). Concretely, workspace detail's last_synced_at filters state=COMPLETED only, so a tenant whose runs always end PARTIAL shows last_synced_at:null in the UI while the agent prompt says 'Data is loaded and ready. Last updated: ...' from a COMPLETED|PARTIAL filter — same data, opposite story. The WorkspaceViewSchema 'usable' predicate also diverges: builders use PROVISIONING during rebuild, the query router accepts only ACTIVE, get_schema_status accepts ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING (a dead arm), so during any multi-tenant rebuild window get_schema_status says not_provisioned (inviting a redundant run_materialization) while the query tool says 'trigger a rebuild' though one is already running. The multi-tenant prompt also collapses vs.state==FAILED into the never-built branch, emitting 'No data has been loaded yet. Call run_materialization' and contradicting PR #229's get_schema_status fix.

votes.evidence:
RE-TRACE 1 — last_synced_at divergence. apps/workspaces/api/workspace_views.py:266-275 (detail view): `MaterializationRun.objects.filter(state=MaterializationRun.RunState.COMPLETED, ...)` → `last_synced_at = latest_completed.isoformat() if latest_completed else None`. Same COMPLETED-only filter in the list view subquery at workspace_views.py:118-125. The multi-tenant prompt builder apps/agents/graph/base.py:356-362 filters `state__in=[COMPLETED, PARTIAL]` and base.py:379 emits `"Data is loaded and ready. Last updated: {last_materialized_at}"`. PARTIAL runs do get completed_at stamped (mcp_server/services/materializer.py:359-370). Reachability of the divergent pair: frontend consumes last_synced_at live — frontend/src/components/ChatEmptyState/ChatEmptyState.tsx:33, WorkspaceSwitcher.tsx:62 ("Has data — synced ..."), and frontend/src/api/workspaces.ts:91 derives "ready" vs "empty" from `ws.last_synced_at != null`. Prompt builders are wired at base.py:758 (single-tenant) and base.py:769 (multi-tenant); WorkspaceDetailView routed at config/urls.py:88.

RE-TRACE 2 — 'usable' predicate divergence during rebuild. Builders set PROVISIONING: apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:286 (`vs.state = SchemaState.PROVISIONING` at start of build_view_schema) and apps/workspaces/services/workspace_service.py:68-71 (`.update(state=SchemaState.PROVISIONING)` then `rebuild_workspace_view_schema.defer(...)`). Query router mcp_server/context.py:114-123 accepts ONLY `state=SchemaState.ACTIVE` and raises `"No active view schema ... Trigger a rebuild via POST /api/workspaces/<id>/tenants/ or a data refresh."` even while a rebuild is already deferred/running. get_schema_status mcp_server/server.py:738-741 filters `state__in=[ACTIVE, MATERIALIZING]`; PROVISIONING misses it, the FAILED check at server.py:749-752 also misses, so server.py:765 returns `not_provisioned` (exists=False) during every rebuild window. Dead arm verified by exhaustive grep: `SchemaState.MATERIALIZING` is NEVER assigned anywhere in apps/ or mcp_server/ (only assignments found: PROVISIONING, ACTIVE, FAILED, TEARDOWN, EXPIRED — schema_manager.py:84,120,180,286,428,436; tasks.py:143,153,182,193,512,542,551,601,605,629,656; workspace_service.py:64,68). So the MATERIALIZING arms at server.py:694, server.py:740, base.py:325, and workspace_service.py:92,102,107 are all dead.

RE-TRACE 3 — FAILED collapsed into never-built branch. base.py:334 `if vs is None or vs.state != SchemaState.ACTIVE:` returns base.py:337-343 "No data has been loaded yet. Call `run_materialization` ..." — this branch swallows FAILED (and PROVISIONING/TEARDOWN/EXPIRED). FAILED is a real reachable vs state: schema_manager.py:428 (build failure) and tasks.py:684-687 (_fail_dependent_view_schemas flips ACTIVE→FAILED when a shared tenant schema is dropped, called from teardown_schema at tasks.py:653). Meanwhile get_schema_status server.py:749-764 (the PR #229 fix) returns `{"exists": True, "state": "failed", "error": ...}` for the same row. Direct contradiction confirmed.

RE-TRACE 4 — derivation count. Independent status/derivation sites found: (1) workspace_views.py:63-106 _schema_status_for_workspaces + _derive_schema_status, (2) workspace_views.py:118-125 list-view last_synced subquery (COMPLETED only), (3) workspace_views.py:266-275 detail last_synced (COMPLETED only), (4) base.py:205-296 single-tenant prompt (TenantSchema ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING; materialized_at via pipeline tables), (5) base.py:306-393 multi-tenant prompt (ACTIVE_STATES runs + COMPLETED|PARTIAL), (6) server.py:652-798 get_schema_status (ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING + COMPLETED|PARTIAL), (7) server.py:408-442 get_materialization_status (raw run state), (8) apps/workspaces/api/views.py:253-263 and 436-440 refresh/tables status (COMPLETED|PARTIAL), (9) tasks.py:997-1000 resume aggregate. "~7 ways" is if anything an undercount.
votes.notes: One mechanism nuance to the headline scenario: for a MULTI-tenant workspace whose runs always end PARTIAL, materialize_workspace gates build_view_schema on all_succeeded (tasks.py:311,322), and a PARTIAL run re-raises (materializer.py:364-371) so success=False — the view schema does NOT become ACTIVE via that path. It becomes ACTIVE via rebuild_workspace_view_schema (tenant add/remove at workspace_service.py:71, or sibling rebuild at tasks.py:346), which only requires ACTIVE TenantSchemas — and provision() marks TenantSchema ACTIVE BEFORE the pipeline runs (schema_manager.py:120). So the "UI null / prompt ready" pair is reachable multi-tenant with one extra (common) step, and is directly reachable single-tenant: provision marks the schema ACTIVE pre-pipeline, a PARTIAL run leaves tables present, the single-tenant prompt (base.py:205+) says "Data is loaded and ready" via pipeline_list_tables (which counts COMPLETED|PARTIAL per mcp_server/services/metadata.py:35,62) while last_synced_at stays null. Second note: during a view-only rebuild there is no active MaterializationRun, so base.py:325's in-progress guard does not fire and the prompt ALSO says "No data has been loaded yet. Call run_materialization" — the redundant-materialization invitation comes from the prompt and get_schema_status simultaneously, and any in-flight-run guard on run_materialization would not block it (no run is active). Window duration is normally seconds but unbounded if the worker is backlogged/dead (a known prod failure mode in this repo's history). Severity/impact as stated (BROKEN-NOW / correctness) is fair: no data loss, but the agent and UI tell users opposite stories from live, routinely-exercised paths.

title: TenantMetadata is per-membership but read with three different scopes: column annotations appear/disappear per user and per surface
claim: The materializer writes TenantMetadata only to the triggering user's membership (OneToOne to TenantMembership); the prompt reads user-scoped (returning None for users who never materialized -> no JSONB column annotations in their prompt), while MCP describe_table/get_metadata and the data dictionary read any membership's row (.afirst()/.first(), arbitrary). The same tenant/table yields different metadata by surface and by user.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain; all five cited locations are accurate.

1. MODEL (one-to-one per membership): apps/workspaces/models.py:266 — `tenant_membership = models.OneToOneField("users.TenantMembership", ... related_name="metadata")`. And TenantMembership has `unique_together = [["user", "tenant"]]` (apps/users/models.py:226) with `tenant = ForeignKey(Tenant, related_name="memberships")` — so ONE tenant has MANY memberships (one per user), each with at most one TenantMetadata row. Multiple divergent rows per tenant are structurally possible.

2. WRITER (triggering membership only): mcp_server/services/materializer.py:539-542 — `_run_discover_phase(tenant_membership, ...)` does `TenantMetadata.objects.update_or_create(tenant_membership=tenant_membership, defaults={"metadata": metadata, ...})`. grep confirms this is the ONLY writer of TenantMetadata in the repo. The membership passed is the one that triggered the run, so only that user's membership gets a row.

3. PROMPT read = USER-scoped: apps/agents/graph/base.py:263-265 — `await TenantMetadata.objects.filter(tenant_membership__tenant=tenant, tenant_membership__user=user).afirst()`. `user` is the requesting user (passed into `_fetch_schema_context(tenant, user)`, base.py:205, called at base.py:758 during prompt assembly). The code itself documents the asymmetry at base.py:134: "Includes user.id because _fetch_schema_context scopes TenantMetadata lookup to the specific user." For a user who never materialized, this returns None.

4. MCP describe_table read = ANY membership in tenant: mcp_server/server.py:210-212 — `TenantMetadata.objects.filter(tenant_membership__tenant_id=ts.tenant_id).afirst()`. get_metadata identical at server.py:270-272. Both are live `@mcp.tool()` agent tools (describe_table at server.py:177, get_metadata at server.py:231).

5. Data dictionary read = ANY membership in tenant: apps/workspaces/api/views.py:194-196 — `_get_tenant_metadata(tenant)` returns `TenantMetadata.objects.filter(tenant_membership__tenant=tenant).first()`. Used at views.py:284 (DataDictionaryView list) and views.py:460 (TableDetailView), both behind the live route `data-dictionary/` (apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:16).

MECHANISM — metadata drives column annotations: All three surfaces pass `tenant_metadata` into mcp_server/services/metadata.py:188 `pipeline_describe_table`, which calls `_build_jsonb_annotations` (metadata.py:235). When tenant_metadata is None it returns `{}` (line 242-243), so the JSONB columns `properties` (on raw_cases) and `form_data` (on raw_forms) get empty `description` (metadata.py:224). The data dictionary path also feeds `_build_source_metadata(table_name, tenant_metadata)` (views.py:289/460). So absence/divergence of the membership row directly changes the column annotations the user/agent sees.

Net: same tenant+table yields (a) NO annotations in the prompt for a user who never personally materialized, but (b) annotations via MCP tools and the data dictionary (which read an arbitrary membership's row). Different by surface and by user — exactly as claimed.
votes.notes:
Two scoping refinements, neither of which weakens the finding:

(1) The user-scoped prompt read (base.py:263) is on the SINGLE-tenant prompt branch (base.py:758, reached when the workspace has exactly one tenant; tenant_count > 1 takes the multi-tenant branch at base.py:760 which does not call _fetch_schema_context). The MCP tools and data dictionary read any-membership regardless of tenant count. So the per-surface divergence holds universally; the per-user prompt gap is specifically the single-tenant case — which is the common case, so impact stands.

(2) No cross-tenant leak: both "any membership" reads are still filtered to the correct tenant (tenant_membership__tenant / tenant_id=ts.tenant_id), just to an arbitrary membership within it. So this is NOT a security issue — impact=correctness and status=DEBT are right. Because provider metadata (case_types/form_definitions) is logically tenant-level rather than user-level, the divergent rows mostly differ in PRESENCE (None for never-materialized users) and STALENESS (.afirst() picks an arbitrary membership's possibly-older row), not in fundamentally different content. That matches the finding's framing ("appear/disappear per user and per surface"). Replication=1 and complexity=accidental are appropriate.

title: pipeline_list_tables is fail-closed for sources but fail-open for dbt models; transient DB error yields a phantom-table or empty catalog that instructs re-materialization
claim: _live_tables_in_schema returns set() on any DB error (docstring promises empty-list-on-error to avoid phantom rows), and pipeline_list_tables correctly drops sources but then lists dbt models optimistically (`if live_table_names and ...`), so on a transient blip the catalog would contain only possibly-nonexistent dbt tables (NOT_FOUND streaks / #190 input shape) — currently moot only because all three pipeline YAMLs declare no transforms.models. When the empty-set path yields zero tables, list_tables decorates it with 'No completed materialization run found. Run run_materialization to load data' inside a success envelope, turning a DB blip into an agent instruction to launch a full expensive re-materialization of healthy data; the same helper backs the data-dictionary API (200s with {tables:{}}) and _get_all_columns returns {} on error.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from the raw files; line numbers in the finding are accurate.

1. Fail-closed helper. mcp_server/services/metadata.py:144-157: `_live_tables_in_schema` wraps the query in `try: result = await _execute_async_parameterized(...) except Exception: ... return set()`. I confirmed the callee does NOT swallow errors — mcp_server/services/query.py:68-93 `_execute_async_parameterized` has no try/except, so a transient `psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect` failure propagates and is caught here, returning an empty set. Docstring at metadata.py:119-120 matches ("Returns an empty set on query failure ... so a transient DB error surfaces as an empty list rather than phantom rows").

2. Sources fail-closed. metadata.py:83 `if physical_name not in live_table_names: continue` — with `live_table_names == set()`, every completed source is dropped.

3. dbt models fail-OPEN. metadata.py:96-100: `for model_name in pipeline_config.dbt_models: if live_table_names and model_name not in live_table_names: continue`. When the set is empty (falsy), the `and` short-circuits to False, `continue` is skipped, and the model is appended unconditionally (lines 101-110). The inline comment at 98-99 documents this intent: "otherwise list them optimistically." So on a blip, the catalog would contain unverified dbt tables — exactly the claim.

4. Currently moot / LATENT. I read all three pipeline YAMLs: pipelines/commcare_sync.yml, connect_sync.yml, ocs_sync.yml — none declare a `transforms:` block. pipeline_registry.py:73-74 `dbt_models` returns `self.transforms.models if self.transforms else []`, so `dbt_models == []` for all three and the optimistic loop body never executes today. The phantom-dbt-table consequence is genuinely latent, as stated.

5. Empty catalog -> re-materialization instruction (LIVE today). server.py:163-167: `note = "No completed materialization run found. Run run_materialization to load data." if not tables else None`, wrapped in `success_response`. apps/agents/prompts/base_system.py:138-144 ("When the Schema is Broken") steers the agent to "Call `run_materialization` to rebuild the data." So an empty list_tables (which a DB blip produces once all sources are dropped and no dbt models exist) is decorated with text that pushes the agent toward a full re-materialization.

6. Reachability — both consumers are live. list_tables is a registered `@mcp.tool()` (server.py:107-173) loaded by the agent graph (apps/agents/mcp_client.py:31). The data-dictionary endpoint is a live route: apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:16 `data-dictionary/` -> config/urls.py:66 (nested under /api/workspaces/<id>/) -> DataDictionaryView._get_from_pipeline (views.py:252) -> async_to_sync(pipeline_list_tables) (views.py:275) and `_get_all_columns` (views.py:282). _get_all_columns returns `{}` on any connection error (views.py:94-96). views.py:278-279 `if not tables_list: return Response({"tables": {}, "generated_at": None})` — a 200 with empty tables on a blip.

7. The empty-set is normally transient, not structural: MANAGED_DATABASE_URL is configured by default (config/settings/base.py:126 + development.py:13-21 derive it; production requires it), so the `if not url` guard (metadata.py:130) is not the usual cause — the `except Exception` around the live query is, matching the "transient blip" framing.

Every link reproduced. The finding's mechanism, line numbers, the moot-via-empty-transforms condition, and reachability all hold.
votes.notes: Minor severity-calibration nuance (not a refutation): the finding bundles two consequences. The phantom-dbt-table outcome is correctly marked LATENT (no pipeline declares transforms). The live-today consequence is the empty-catalog -> re-materialization-instruction path, which is real and reachable now. The cost-perf impact (a transient blip triggering an expensive re-materialization of healthy data) is plausible but probabilistic: the prompt steers the agent toward run_materialization on an empty/broken catalog, yet a single empty list_tables could alternatively be surfaced to the user (base_system.py:143-144 offers "ask whether to re-materialize" as an option). So the cost-perf harm is real but agent-behavior-dependent rather than guaranteed. This does not change the verdict — the code-level defect (asymmetric fail-closed/fail-open + empty-catalog note inside a success envelope) is exactly as described. Keeping impact=cost-perf and status=LATENT for the headline (phantom-table) claim is defensible; one could argue the empty-catalog->note sub-path is BROKEN-NOW-eligible since it requires no latent precondition.

title: Artifact versioning copies full code per update and soft-delete never frees rows; live artifacts re-execute all source queries serially per open with no caching
claim: Artifact.create_new_version writes a full new row (entire React code + data JSON) per update linked via parent_artifact, and deletion is soft with an undelete endpoint so rows are never physically removed — agents iterating on a dashboard accrete a full-source copy per iteration indefinitely. ArtifactQueryDataView executes each source_query serially, each opening a fresh TLS connection and re-running full sqlglot validation, with no result caching or conditional-request support, so a 5-query dashboard costs 5 sequential connect+validate+execute cycles per viewer per open. (Artifact.create_new_version is also a dead duplicate of the inline version-bump in artifact_tool.)

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every link of the chain by reading the files directly; line numbers in the finding are accurate.

1. PER-UPDATE FULL-ROW COPY (versioning):
- `apps/artifacts/models.py:173-197` `create_new_version` constructs a brand-new `Artifact(...)` copying `code`, `data`, `source_queries` and `version=self.version + 1`, `parent_artifact=self`, then `.save()`. Full duplicate row.
- The LIVE path is the inline duplicate in `apps/agents/tools/artifact_tool.py:296-311`: `update_artifact` builds `Artifact(workspace=..., code=code, data=..., version=original.version + 1, parent_artifact=original, ...)` and `await new_artifact.asave()`. This is the agent tool actually wired to the graph, so every agent-driven iteration on a dashboard writes a new full-source row. Confirmed the entire React `code` text and `data`/`source_queries` JSON are copied per version.

2. SOFT-DELETE NEVER FREES ROWS:
- `models.py:16-18` `SoftDeleteManager` filters `is_deleted=False`; `models.py:147-159` `soft_delete`/`undelete` only flip the flag.
- `views.py:915-920` DELETE calls `artifact.soft_delete(...)`; `views.py:923-932` `ArtifactUndeleteView` (route `urls.py:24-28`) restores via `Artifact.all_objects`. No DELETE route ever calls `.delete()`.
- I searched the whole tree: the only hard `.delete()`/purge is `apps/workspaces/management/commands/purge_synced_data.py`, whose own docstring (line 17) says "Chat, artifacts, and learnings are preserved." There is no scheduled task, management command, or cron that physically removes artifact rows. So rows accrete indefinitely. Confirmed.

3. SERIAL RE-EXECUTION PER OPEN, NO CACHING:
- Route `urls.py:39-43` `<uuid:artifact_id>/query-data/` -> `ArtifactQueryDataView` (`views.py:764-843`).
- Reachable from two live callers: the sandbox iframe JS (`views.py:254`, fired whenever `has_live_queries`) AND the frontend `ArtifactPanel.tsx:57` (`api.get(.../query-data/)`). So it runs on every artifact open by every viewer.
- `views.py:814-822`: `for i, entry in enumerate(artifact.source_queries): ... result = await execute_query(ctx, sql)` — a plain serial loop, awaited one at a time. No `asyncio.gather`, no batching.
- `mcp_server/services/query.py:106-129` `execute_query`: builds a fresh `SQLValidator` (line 108), calls `validator.validate(sql)` which at `sql_validator.py:175` does `sqlglot.parse(sql, ...)` fresh every call (no `@lru_cache`/memoization anywhere), then `_execute_async` (line 38-43) does `await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect(**ctx.connection_params, ...)` — a brand-new connection per query (QueryContext holds raw `connection_params`, no pool). So a 5-query dashboard = 5 sequential connect + full-validate + execute cycles per viewer per open. No result cache, no ETag/Last-Modified/conditional-request handling on the view. Confirmed.

4. DEAD DUPLICATE:
- `grep` for callers of `create_new_version` across all `*.py`: zero call sites (only the definition). `get_version_history` IS used (`apps/artifacts/admin.py:129`), but `create_new_version` is genuinely dead and is a behavioral duplicate of the inline block in `artifact_tool.py:296-311`. Confirmed.
votes.notes: Status DEBT / impact cost-perf is correct — these are real inefficiencies/accretion, not a live functional break. Two minor refinements (do not change the verdict): (a) The per-open cost is slightly understated for a fresh open: before the serial loop the view also does one schema-touch ORM round-trip (`views.py:809-812 ts.atouch()`) plus `load_tenant_context`; so it's N connect+validate+execute cycles PLUS a couple of bookkeeping round-trips. (b) `execute_query` returns a structured error dict rather than raising on bad SQL, so a malformed source_query degrades to a per-query error entry rather than aborting the batch — doesn't affect the cost claim. (c) The finding lists `models.py:16` for the SoftDeleteManager and `models.py:173-197` for create_new_version — both exact. Reachability is solid: the query-data endpoint is hit by BOTH the sandbox iframe and the React ArtifactPanel on every open; update_artifact is a live agent tool; undelete is a live route (though no frontend caller found — operator/API only, which is consistent with rows-never-freed).
